boostorg/boost_install#12.body:
Boost 1.70's cmake config makes `cmake` stop looking where I tell it to in two ways:

1. With Boost 1.68, I could tell `cmake` the location of the Boost I wanted to use with either a `BOOST_ROOT` environment variable or a `-DBOOST_ROOT=[...]` command line flag but with 1.70.0, the latter option has stopped working.
1. If I have a Boost 1.70 directory in `/opt/`, `cmake` uses that one instead of the Boost (either 1.68 or 1.70) in another location that I explicitly request (via either a `BOOST_ROOT` environment variable or a `-DBOOST_ROOT=[...]` command line flag). Even if I have two Boost 1.70 directories in `/opt/`, `cmake` chooses which one to use itself, disregarding my explicit choice.

I have seen these issues with `cmake-3.12.1-Linux-x86_64` and `cmake-3.15.0-rc3-Linux-x86_64`.

Apologies: I'm not sure whether this issue should be raised here or against `FindBoost.cmake` on https://gitlab.kitware.com/cmake — I'm guessing here because the problem appears to be associated with a change in the Boost version not the CMake version. Sorry if that's incorrect.
boostorg/boost_install#12.title: Boost 1.70's cmake config makes cmake stop looking where I tell it to

envoyproxy/envoy#20190.body:
**Title:** Fix Deprecated Tap Request Examples

**Description:** The [Tap filter docs](https://www.envoyproxy.io/docs/envoy/latest/configuration/http/http_filters/tap_filter.html?highlight=tap) contain example POST bodies which contain the deprecated `match_config` field. In addition to this field being deprecated, utilization of this field causes test failures in Envoy's CI flow. 

**Fix:** Replace all instances of `match_config` in the Tap documentation with `match`

*Title*: *clarification around jw_authn metrics through docs*

*Description*:

Started using envoy's [jwt_authn http filter](https://www.envoyproxy.io/docs/envoy/latest/api-v3/extensions/filters/http/jwt_authn/v3/config.proto.html) and was tracking down some additional stuff for metrics.

```
http.ingress_http.jwt_authn.jwks_fetch_failed
```

envoyproxy/envoy#29758.body:
*Title*: *Support parsing data from server-side events (SSE) and add to metadata*

*Description*:
This issue is more to get opinions on how to approach the issue. I am happy to work on implementation.
TLDR: We want to support parsing SSE events that can be used generically while supporting our specific use case.

Context: We have a use case that involves internally using the OpenAI API and using Envoy to send the request to OpenAI servers. The responses from OpenAI API are sometimes in SSE format. See example below for SSE response that holds JSON events:

```
data: {
  "choices": [],
  "created": 1677825464,
  "id": "...",
  "model": "gpt-3.5-turbo-0301",
  "object": "chat.completion.chunk"
}
data: {
  "choices": [],
  "created": 1677825464,
  "id": "...",
  "model": "gpt-3.5-turbo-0301",
  "object": "chat.completion.chunk"
}
data: {
  "choices": [],
  "usage": {"prompt_tokens": 10, "completion_tokens": 10, "total_tokens": 20},
  "created": 1677825464,
  "id": "...",
  "model": "gpt-3.5-turbo-0301",
  "object": "chat.completion.chunk"
}
```
Note each "chunk" or event is a JSON. We want a filter that can parse one of the chunks and extract the `total_tokens` which is in the last chunk inside "usage" and add to metadata.

SSE specifications specifies that each event is passed via `data: "some value"` where the value is of type string. Our use case, the type is actually a JSON. 

How would we support parsing SSE given above info?

I am thinking of several approach here:
1) Update HTTP **json_to_metadata** filter to also parse SSE if found `content-type: text/event-stream`. 
pros: already has json parsing logic so no need for duplicate code. 
cons: too specific that it only handles parsing JSON from SSE since SSE events can hold any string (i.e non-JSON).

2) Create a separate filter (stream_to_metadata?) that parses SSE and adds to metadata. We can make this filter more generic to support extraction of data of non-JSON. 
pros: Can be more generic to support non-JSON types.
cons: Likely has duplicate code with json_to_metadata to handle JSON parsing.

cc: @JuniorHsu, @kbaichoo

[optional *Relevant Links*:]
Extra note for OpenAI API users: The usage token in SSE is an early alpha feature that needs to be turned on in your OpenAI organization account else it will not be in the event chunks.
envoyproxy/envoy#29758.title: Support parsing data from server-side events (SSE) and add to metadata

envoyproxy/envoy#31777.body:
*Title*: Opentelemetry Tracer: missing log events

*Description*:
The lightstep tracer used to log span logs (events) as part of its operation. It included information like timing for when the client connected, timing when the first header was received, and, importantly, timing when the last data was received.  It was super useful to have this breakdown for troubleshooting, because it would allow finding out if the client is slow sending data.

The opentelementry trace doesn't seem to have this information. Can you please consider adding it?

Thank you

[optional *Relevant Links*:]
>Any extra documentation required to understand the issue.
envoyproxy/envoy#31777.title: Opentelemetry Tracer: missing log events

envoyproxy/envoy#35152.body:
*Title*: *Support to weaken strong ETags when compressing/decompressing*

*Description*:
Currently, when using the compressor filter, there are basically two options to handle (strong) ETags:
- when setting `disable_on_etag_header: true` then do not compress a response
- when setting `disable_on_etag_header: false` then throw away the strong ETag (only keep weak ETags)

But one other option, that is popular in other HTTP proxies/caches, for example Varnish HTTP Cache, is:
- weaken any strong ETag by prepending it with the "W/" prefix when compressing a response before sending it to the client

When searching the web, there are _numerous_ stackoverflow, bugzilla (or other bug tracking tools) and blog posts about how to handle ETags and how to do it in the least surprising ways (surprising for any developer or server/platform operator).

Now, since we were using Varnish and I was familiar with the "weaken any strong ETag when compressing" behaviour, it caught me by surprise that the Envoy compressor does _not_ provide such an option (only the above two mentioned options).

My current use-case is to compress/decompress the traffic between each two Istio sidecars when the backends do not:
- advertize in client requests that they support compressiong via Accept-Encoding
- compress the response themselves when the client did advertize support for it and then adding a Content-Encoding response header

Would it be possible to provide a way to weaken any strong ETag with Envoy's compressor here?
envoyproxy/envoy#35152.title: Support to weaken strong ETags when compressing/decompressing

envoyproxy/envoy#36442.body:
*Title*: Set ratelimiting to all cidrs with exceptions

*Description*:

I have usecase that I should add ratelimiting to all addresses (0.0.0.0/0) EXCEPT few "whitelisted ips". As I see it, it is quite common use case that people want to set rate limits to everything except few internal api ips (whitelist services that should work always, no matter what).

*Relevant Links*:
https://www.envoyproxy.io/docs/envoy/latest/api-v3/config/route/v3/route_components.proto#envoy-v3-api-msg-config-route-v3-ratelimit-action

currently envoy does not support exceptions to the `remote_address` matcher. But it could have "not logic"?
envoyproxy/envoy#36442.title: Possible to set "whitelisted ips" in ratelimiting

envoyproxy/envoy#37577.body:
Description:

local_replay_config allows to change response code, body and other local replay parameters
unfortunately using the RESPONSE_CODE in the body will use the override value, the original response value is not present anymore.

```
        local_reply_config:
            mappers:
            - filter:
                status_code_filter:
                  comparison:
                   op: EQ
                   value:
                     default_value: 400
                     runtime_key: "%RESPONSE_CODE%"
              status_code: 500
              body_format_override:
                text_format: "Original code: %RESPONSE_CODE%"
```

In this example the body will be:
```
Original code: 500
```
instead of
```
Original code: 400
```

Present in version 1.33
envoyproxy/envoy#37577.title: local_replay_config RESPONSE_CODE is override before body_format

*Title*: bazel: build failure when using envoy as an external repository

*Description*:
When using envoy as an external repository such as in [envoy-filter-example](https://github.com/envoyproxy/envoy-filter-example), building `@envoy//source/...` fails

It looks like `crates_repository` is somehow looking for the lockfiles in the root repository rather than within the `@envoy` repository. CC: @mathetake

```
git clone https://github.com/envoyproxy/envoy-filter-example.git
cd envoy-filter-example
git submodule --update
sed -i 's/{ENVOY_SRCDIR}/envoy/' WORKSPACE
bazel build @envoy//source/...
```

envoyproxy/envoy#39174.body:
# Objective We’d like to initiate an issue and discussion around supporting the [Model Context Protocol (MCP)](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/introduction) in Envoy as a gateway. # What is MCP? MCP is an open, stateless/stateful protocol that allows GenAI applications to retrieve and exchange context (e.g. source code, files, documents) with LLMs, using JSON-RPC semantics. A significant MCP [streamable HTTP](https://spec.modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-03-26) update last month, introduces OAuth 2.1-based authorization, streamable HTTP transport, JSON-RPC batching, and tool annotations. This is a major update that can make MCP work as a remote server. # Details MCP can use transports such as stdio or [Streamable HTTP](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/specification/2025-03-26/basic/transports#streamable-http). With the streamable HTTP update, the bidirectional JSON-RPC messages can be exchanged over HTTP POST and GET. And SSE is wrapped into the streamable HTTP. The following diagrams show the HTTP transport and capacity negotiation in MCP:

Transport
Capacity Negotiation
This proposal explores how Envoy can serve as a gateway between MCP clients and servers — helping route, process, and secure MCP messages in a scalable and extensible way. # Design Proposal With MCP gaining traction as a standard way for AI tools to interact with contextual data, we believe Envoy can play an important role in enabling infrastructure-level routing, load balancing, and observability for these interactions. This issue proposes a set of functions that enables Envoy to act as a gateway between MCP clients and servers, covering the following use cases, in order of their complexity and implementation: ## Proposed Functionality 1. MCP session aware load balancing based on the MCP endpoint (HTTP request URI). * This work is already in progress in Envoy https://github.com/envoyproxy/envoy/pull/39004, thanks to @wbpcode 2. Parsing of MCP protocol to make Envoy aware of MCP request properties such as method/id, call arguments, or return values. 3. Authentication of MCP requests using O-Auth2, JWT or API keys based on MCP request properties. 4. Authorization of MCP requests and messages using RBAC (for example authorizing specific MCP methods based on the caller identity). This authorization will apply to both client and server requests. 5. Transcoding JSON-RPC messages to existing API surfaces, for example gRPC and OpenAPI. 6. Rate limiting of MCP requests. 7. Customizable business logic for MCP messages, similar to HTTP filters, including remote callouts for MCP messages. 8. Load balancing and fanning-out of individual MCP messages (i.e. based on method) from a single HTTP stream. 9. Gateway initialized SSE stream support with session resumption and JSON-RPC batch support. # Note MCP is closely related to the [A2A protocol](https://google.github.io/A2A/#/documentation) that is proposed for agent to agent communications. Both protocols use JSON-RPC and streaming semantics and stateful sessions. While this proposal is covering MCP, it does not preclude extending the same functions to A2A protocol. While some of the functions are agnostic of the underlying protocol, business logic specific to A2A can be implemented in its own extension, sharing common implementation, such as JSON-RPC parser and framing, with MCP. # Acknowledgments This proposal was framed collaboratively with @htuch, @yanavlasov, and @botengyao. This issue is intended to surface the proposal for public discussion, gather feedback, and coordinate OSS collaboration. We welcome thoughts, feedback, and ideas from the community as we continue to iterate on this direction.
envoyproxy/envoy#39174.title: Proposal: Envoy Support for Model Context Protocol (MCP)

envoyproxy/envoy#40053.body:
## Title: Support Degraded Header in Outlier Detection

## Description
Envoy currently allows upstream endpoints to signal a degraded health state via the `x-envoy-degraded` header during active health checks.
However,  outlier detection a.k.a passive health checking does not currently interpret this header. If an upstream service returns an HTTP 200 response with `x-envoy-degraded: true` header, outlier detection still considers this a successful response and does not change the health status of the endpoint.

## Request
Extend outlier detection to optionally treat responses containing `x-envoy-degraded` as a signal to mark the endpoint as degraded, even if the status code is 200. This would allow degraded state signaling to be honored in both active and passive health checks. Specifically, if `x-envoy-degraded` is observed in a successful response (e.g., HTTP 200), outlier detection marks the endpoint as degraded. This feature could be enabled via a new boolean flag in the outlier detection config.
envoyproxy/envoy#40053.title: Support Degraded Header in Outlier Detection

*Title*: *ext_ proc: FULL DUPLEX STREAMED response body truncated when multiple filters defined *

*Description*:
envoy version: 1.35.4

Two ext-proc filters are defined:
1.  ext-proc-1: handles response headers
2.  ext-proc-2: handles response headers and body (FULL_DUPLEX_STREAMED)

When body responses are returned with some delay, there are sporadic failures, where:
- The response body is truncated, see example in logs: `UPSTREAM_WIRE_BYTES_RECEIVED:50730 DOWNSTREAM_WIRE_BYTES_SENT:4151` 
- The ext-proc server encounters an error when sending a response: `send error rpc error: code = Canceled desc = context canceled`

Additional info:
- When disabling ext-proc-1, issue does not reproduce. Is it possible that messages/processing mode of ext-proc-1 influences behavior of ext-proc-2? 
- From the logs, it seems that the issue is triggered when the payload is split into multiple chunks instead of being sent as a single chunk. 

*Config*:

run ext_proc and echo backend:
- simple ext proc: https://gist.github.com/guydc/2cb8fbb368c90d234b8a9a95d5b99336
- simple echo server: https://gist.github.com/guydc/a6b069cf7951767a151b6ce53c7d80ed

envoyproxy/envoy#42682.body:
*Title*: tls: Add certificate compression (RFC 8879) support for TCP TLS with brotli/zstd algorithms

*Description*:
Envoy currently supports TLS certificate compression (RFC 8879) only for QUIC, and only with the zlib algorithm. This limits effectiveness because:

1. TCP TLS has no certificate compression support - The majority of TLS connections use TCP, not QUIC
2. QUIC only supports zlib - Chrome/Chromium browsers only advertise brotli for certificate compression, meaning QUIC certificate compression is ineffective for the largest browser segment

In production traffic analysis, TLS handshake data (primarily certificates) accounts for ~32% of total data transfer. This is especially impactful for:
- iOS clients which have limited TLS session resumption support
- Mobile apps that frequently reconnect (app backgrounding, network changes)
- Cloud cost optimization - AWS charges for total Data Transfer Out including all TLS overhead

Browser Certificate Compression Support (RFC 8879):

| Browser                      | Supported Algorithms |
|------------------------------|----------------------|
| Chrome/Edge/Opera (Chromium) | brotli only          |
| Firefox                      | zlib, brotli, zstd   |
| Safari                       | zlib                 |

Current QUIC implementation: source/common/quic/cert_compression.cc

*Relevant Links*:
- https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc8879
- https://commondatastorage.googleapis.com/chromium-boringssl-docs/ssl.h.html#SSL_CTX_add_cert_compression_alg
envoyproxy/envoy#42682.title: tls: Add certificate compression (RFC 8879) support for TCP TLS with brotli/zstd algorithms

envoyproxy/envoy#42869.body:
*Title*: 
TLS inspector should populate `REQUESTED_SERVER_NAME` (access log) with SNI for TLS handshake errors

*Description*:
Initially TLS errors from TLS-inspector were not propagated to `DOWNSTREAM_TRANSPORT_FAILURE_REASON` to be logged via access log (https://github.com/istio/istio/issues/58281, @botengyao thankfully fixed this via https://github.com/envoyproxy/envoy/pull/42034. I just rolled out Istio 1.28.2 (with Envoy 1.36 as istio-proxy) containing the fix and am seeing this field populated now!

While the particular failure reason is now available, unfortunately there is no information on the SNI the TLS client sent to the server. This would be incredibly helpful to further narrow down which clients have TLS issues (which service they are actually trying to reach). On successful connections this info should be in `%REQUESTED_SERVER_NAME(X:Y)%` (https://www.envoyproxy.io/docs/envoy/latest/configuration/advanced/substitution_formatter.html)


I dug into the source code of the tls_inspector at e.g. https://github.com/botengyao/envoy/blob/06fb3da5fd0e8a3db1ae4cb511aa7a78a5d0422c/source/extensions/filters/listener/tls_inspector/tls_inspector.cc#L212-L229 to see that there already is quite a lot of distinction on how far the Client Hello could be parsed and potentially be accepted. 

There is the extraction of the server name (that must be the SNI, reading https://docs.openssl.org/3.6/man3/SSL_CTX_set_tlsext_servername_callback/) and there is a Filter already extracting and using the server name for all sorts ... https://github.com/botengyao/envoy/blob/06fb3da5fd0e8a3db1ae4cb511aa7a78a5d0422c/source/extensions/filters/listener/tls_inspector/tls_inspector.cc#L141-L151

so it should be possible to also send this info to have it end up populating the `REQUESTED_SERVER_NAME` field, even (or especially) for TLS handshake errors. The info is also already logged via Envoy debug log in https://github.com/botengyao/envoy/blob/06fb3da5fd0e8a3db1ae4cb511aa7a78a5d0422c/source/extensions/filters/listener/tls_inspector/tls_inspector.cc#L145.

^^ @ggreenway you reviewed the fix, thus I am mentioning you here.
envoyproxy/envoy#42869.title: TLS inspector should populate `REQUESTED_SERVER_NAME` (access log) with SNI for TLS handshake errors

envoyproxy/envoy#42947.body:
MaxMind provides two ways to get the data about an IP's service provider. From the free ASN database and the paid ISP database. In most cases organization name is enough, and there is no need to use the ISP db for more precise data.
ASN database contains two fields:
`{
    "autonomous_system_number": 
      12345 <uint32>
    "autonomous_system_organization": 
      "Amazon-02" <utf8_string>
  }`
It is currently possible to get just the ID and not the human-readable name.
This data could be very helpful. For technical domains, we can limit access to webhook services (eg. GitHub, BitBucket, clouds). On the other hand, for public use domains, we can block bulletproof providers and/or VPNs.

Possible solutions:

- make isp field more versatile and get org data from ASN if ISP db wasn't mounted;
- add new key for asn_org;
- change asn key to return org name instead of number (I might be wrong here, but I see no use for it outside of the geoprocessing scope)
envoyproxy/envoy#42947.title: Enrich MaxMind's GeoIP module with organization info

envoyproxy/envoy#43116.body:
**Description:**

_Prerequisites:_

- The cluster has a TLS transport socket configured for upstream connections.
- The TLS transport socket references a client certificate via SDS.
- The cluster has active health checking enabled.

All of these must be true for the bug to happen. If removing any of them, the problem goes away.

Control plane ([Contour](https://github.com/projectcontour/contour)) uses basic xDS with SotW updates (separate gRPC streams for each resource type).

_The Problem:_

The upstream service is unavailable for certain period of time:

- **Scenario 1:** The client gets a `503 Service Unavailable` "no healthy upstream" error for 4 minutes after Envoy restarts
  - This is counted from the moment Envoy is back up and ready with all configuration from XDS. It doesn't include the time Envoy takes to be ready serve requests.
  - The downtime lasts for `healthy_threshold` * `no_traffic_interval` (for example: 4 * 60s = 4min).
- **Scenario 2:** The client gets a `503 Service Unavailable` "no healthy upstream" error for several seconds after rotating the Envoy client certificate.
  - The downtime lasts for `healthy_threshold` * `interval` seconds (for example: 4 * 5s = 20s).

I don't know exactly which Envoy versions are affected, but this isn't just a problem in the newest code. It's not specific to any recent version.

I do not have a complete understanding of the root cause yet, but the issue appears to be related to the order in which xDS resources are delivered and processed. Envoy handles the request forwarding correctly regardless the delivery order. However, active health checks fail if the SDS secret isn't ready when the cluster and endpoints are created. This happens even if the secrets arrive just a few milliseconds later. It seems there isn't any special retry logic for this. Instead, it just acts like the upstream host is actually down.

My current theory is as follows:

**Scenario 1:** 4 minutes of unavailability after Envoy restart

Envoy is restarted and it connects to the xDS server to fetch its configuration:

1. Cluster is created via CDS with:
   - Active health checking enabled
   - TLS transport socket with SDS-referenced client certificate
2. Cluster endpoints are added via EDS
3. Health checker is started
   - Endpoints are marked unhealthy because the SDS secret is not yet available
4. SDS discovery request is sent by Envoy and the secret is delivered by the control plane
5. Cluster enters warming state and it starts sending health check probes
   - Upstream respond with success and probes succeed
   - Upstream remains unhealthy until `healthy_threshold` * `no_traffic_interval` (for example: 4 * 60s = 4min)
6. Cluster becomes healthy and client requests start to succeed

During this time (about 4 minutes) traffic is rejected with `503 Service Unavailable` "no healthy upstream".

Note: If triggering a cluster update during this period, then upstream becomes available again immediately.


**Scenario 2:** 20 seconds of unavailability after client certificate rotation

The cluster is updated with a new SDS secret reference due to certificate rotation:

1. Cluster is updated via CDS with
   - Active health checking enabled
   - TLS transport socket with new SDS reference
2. Old cluster is removed and new cluster is created
3. There is no EDS update since endpoints remain the same
4. Client requests still succeed for a while (~6 seconds but it varies)
5. Health checker for new cluster is started
   - Endpoints are marked unhealthy because the SDS secret is not yet available
   - Client requests start to fail with `503 Service Unavailable` "no healthy upstream"
6. New SDS secret is delivered
7. Cluster re-enters warming state and it starts sending health check probes
   - Upstream respond with success and probes succeed
   - Upstream remains unhealthy until `healthy_threshold` * `interval` (for example: 4 * 5s = 20s)
8. Cluster becomes healthy and client requests start to succeed

During this time (about 20 seconds) traffic is rejected with `503 Service Unavailable` "no healthy upstream".

**Repro steps:**

It's hard to provide steps to reproduce this, as it requires XDS server code. You can find the steps for Contour here: https://github.com/projectcontour/contour/issues/7384.

**Admin and Stats Output:**

Stats are included in attached logs.

**Config:**

See the XDS gRPC captures attached below.


**Logs:**

I'm attaching gRPC captures taken from control plane side of the xDS connection (Contour) using my own capture tool. They can be viewed just as plain json file or with the browser based [grpc-json-sniffer](https://github.com/tsaarni/grpc-json-sniffer) app.

* [troubleshooting-grpc-capture-certificate-rotation.json](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/24799892/troubleshooting-grpc-capture-certificate-rotation.json) xDS message dump, taken from certificate rotation test (traffic disturbance for 20 seconds) 
* [troubleshooting-logs-certificate-rotation.txt](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/24799916/troubleshooting-logs-certificate-rotation.txt) logs, stats etc, taken from certificate rotation test (which caused traffic disturbance)
* [troubleshooting-grpc-capture-envoy-restart.json](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/24799928/troubleshooting-grpc-capture-envoy-restart.json)  xDS message dump taken after Envoy restart (traffic disturbance for 4 minutes after full configuration has been received by Envoy) 
* [troubleshooting-grpc-capture-initial-httproxy-creation.json](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/24799936/troubleshooting-grpc-capture-initial-httproxy-creation.json) xDS message dump from initial/successful cluster creation (no disturbance) 
* [troubleshooting-envoy-restart.txt](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/24816796/troubleshooting-envoy-restart.txt) logs (no stats) from Envoy restart (which caused traffic disturbance). These logs have some additional debug prints I've added (lines with `[SDS-DEBUG]` prefix)

envoyproxy/envoy#43268.body: This proposal presents the high-level design and ideas for supporting Agent2Agent(A2A) protocol in Envoy # Background ## What is A2A? [Agent2Agent(or A2A) protocol ](https://a2a-protocol.org/latest/) is an open protocol designed for dynamic and multi-modal agent collaboration. It aims to enable interoperability between different agent systems, even those built with varying frameworks or by different vendors. The protocol supports capabilities discovery, communication modalities (text, forms, media) negotiation, task management, and agent collaboration. It is built on existing standards like HTTP, SSE, and JSON-RPC, and emphasizes enterprise-grade security and privacy. ## A2A and MCP A2A ❤️ MCP: They are complementary Protocols for Agentic Systems. An agentic application might primarily use A2A to communicate with other agents. Each individual agent internally uses MCP to interact with its specific tools and resources. # Design Details At a high-level, the Envoy pattern for A2A can be a mixture of : - Parsing: JSON-RPC Parser parses the request message (either for MCP or A2A) to extract necessary attributes - Authentication: Validate the authentication of each request based on http header and/or own authentication requirement (e.g., SPIFEE ID) - Authorization: Authorize the request specific to the agent's implementation, the data it handles, and applicable enterprise policies - Observability: Leverage [OpenTelemetry support](https://www.envoyproxy.io/docs/envoy/latest/api-v3/config/trace/v3/opentelemetry.proto.html) in Envoy for distributed tracing and metrics collection. ## Parsing A2A supports 3 major protocol bindings: JSON-RPC, gRPC, and HTTP/REST. Today, Envoy natively provides first class support for [gRPC](https://www.envoyproxy.io/docs/envoy/latest/intro/arch_overview/other_protocols/grpc) and HTTP/REST (e.g., RESTful routing, diverse REST verbs, and authentication mechanism). This part focuses on the parsing mechanism for JSON-RPC, which follows [JSON-RPC 2.0](https://www.jsonrpc.org/) specification. JSON-RPC 2.0 lacks a direct mapping to HTTP semantics, such as method names are contained within the payload. Consequently, Envoy must perform streaming JSON parsing to extract these necessary attributes. To streamline this, we propose a unified parsing architecture: a shared JSON-RPC library will handle core payload extraction for both A2A and MCP, while protocol-specific layers handle their respective unique attributes. The architecture can be illustrated as diagram below: ## Agent Card Support AgentCard is a plain JSON file that details agents’ specific functionalities. It enables agents to discover and understand an agent's specific capabilities. A2A support three discovery strategies with GET requests: 1) Well-Known URI 2) Curated Registries 3) Private Discovery 1. Route to agent card service The agent card is at https:///.well-known/agent-card.json and can be retrieved via a GET request. Envoy, as a proxy, handles the routing and cluster selection by its router filter based on route match, like the config example below: ``` virtual_hosts: - name: agent_service domains: [ "base_url:*"] routes: - match: path: "/.well-known/agent-card.json" headers: - name: ":method" exact_match: "GET" route: cluster: agent_card_backend clusters: - name: agent_card_backend ..... ``` Since agent cards are usually small and bounded, we can leverage existing JSON parsing ability(Envoy::Json::Factory) to parse the agent card response. Then we can identify the URL for routing and security requirements for authentication/authorization. 2. Agent Card Registry It can be achieved via xDS or ext_proc. For example, the control plane or ext_proc server acts as the "Source of Truth" for the Agent Cards. When a new agent is registered, the control Plane sends a DiscoveryResponse containing the new Agent Card JSON or ext_proc server sends a ProcessingResponse. 3. Static Agent Card Options described above are dynamic approaches. For simple deployment or the production environment where the agents are part of the infrastructure, the agent card can be provided via static config. For example, embedding the agent card into the direct response for that route ``` virtual_hosts: name: agent_service domains: ["*"] routes: # 1. Direct Response for Agent Card Discovery - match: path: "/.well-known/agent-card.json" direct_response: status: 200 body: # Valid A2A Agent Card JSON inline_string: | { "name": "finance-agent", "description": "Market analysis agent", "url": "https://api.yourdomain.com/v1/a2a", "version": "1.0.0", "capabilities": { "streaming": true }, "skills": [ { "name": "get_stock_price", "description": "Returns current price for a ticker" } ] } # Required headers for A2A compliance response_headers_to_add: - header: { key: "Content-Type", value: "application/json" } - header: { key: "Access-Control-Allow-Origin", value: "*" } ``` ## Authentication and Authorization A2A (or Agentic AI protocols in general) leverages well-established web security standards. Within this framework, identity details are managed at the protocol layer. More specifically in A2A, remote agents advertise its capabilities, identity, and requirements via [Agent Card](https://a2a-protocol.org/latest/tutorials/python/3-agent-skills-and-card/#agent-card). It supports various standard mechanisms below: - mTLS - OAuth 2.0 ([RFC6749](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc6749)) - OpenID Connect (OIDC) - API Keys The high-level system design for the aforementioned security and authorization mechanisms can be achieved by leveraging Envoy’s enterprise-grade extension ecosystem- such as SPIFFE CertValidator, ext_authz, and authentication filters (JWT/OAuth 2.0) etc - supplemented by targeted enhancements (e.g., https://github.com/envoyproxy/envoy/issues/41974) For example, the sequence diagram for Identity-Based Security via SPIFFE-mTLS and Policy Enforcement: # Acknowledgments This proposal incorporates discussion and input from @botengyao @abeyad @yanavlasov @yangfanud We highly value the community's perspective and welcome further insights, feedback, and ideas as we refine our strategy, evolve the design, and iron out the remaining details.
envoyproxy/envoy#43268.title: Design Proposal: Agent2Agent(A2A) Support in Envoy

envoyproxy/envoy#43310.body: # Description: Envoy v1.37 introduces the `append_action` ([`config.core.v3.HeaderValueOption.HeaderAppendAction`](https://www.envoyproxy.io/docs/envoy/latest/api-v3/config/core/v3/base.proto#envoy-v3-api-enum-config-core-v3-headervalueoption-headerappendaction)) for ext_authz. Previously, only `append` was supported. This `append` option's default value is `true`, however, within a ext_authz [`CheckResponse`](https://www.envoyproxy.io/docs/envoy/latest/api-v3/service/auth/v3/external_auth.proto#envoy-v3-api-msg-service-auth-v3-checkresponse) it defaults to `false`: > The [external authorization service](https://www.envoyproxy.io/docs/envoy/latest/api-v3/service/auth/v3/external_auth.proto#envoy-v3-api-msg-service-auth-v3-checkresponse) and [external processor service](https://www.envoyproxy.io/docs/envoy/latest/api-v3/service/ext_proc/v3/external_processor.proto#envoy-v3-api-msg-service-ext-proc-v3-processingresponse) have default value (false) for this field. Up until v1.37 leaving this option unset caused a header value _not_ to be appended. In v1.37 we started seeing header values being appended that were previously not appended. I believe this bug was introduced with #42878. # Example: v1.36.3 ``` Accept: */* Accept-Encoding: gzip, br Authorization: Bearer <> ``` v1.37.0 ``` Accept: */* Accept-Encoding: gzip, br Authorization: Bearer <>,Bearer <> ``` Our ext-authz server is implemented in Go, and we specifically do not set the `Append` option when mutating headers in the checkResponse, the value is `nil`. According to the documentation, this should be interpreted as `false`. ```golang corev3.HeaderValueOption{ Header: &corev3.HeaderValue{ Key: strings.ToLower(k), Value: v, }, } ``` Attaching a debugger to our ext_authz server also shows that `Append` is never set: We can also see, that `AppendAction` is implicitly set to `HeaderValueOption_APPEND_IF_EXISTS_OR_ADD(0)`, which is the [documented behaviour](https://www.envoyproxy.io/docs/envoy/latest/api-v3/config/core/v3/base.proto#envoy-v3-api-msg-config-core-v3-headervalueoption). This indicates to me that the absence of an explicit value for `append` is falsely interpreted as `true` instead of as `false`, which causes the new implementation to use `APPEND_IF_EXISTS_OR_ADD` instead of the backwards compatible `OVERWRITE_IF_EXISTS_OR_ADD`: ```cpp if (header.has_append()) { // Handle deprecated append boolean for backward compatibility. // When from_deprecated_append is true and action is APPEND_IF_EXISTS_OR_ADD, // the filter will use appendCopy() instead of addCopy(). return {header.append().value() ? HeaderValueOption::APPEND_IF_EXISTS_OR_ADD : HeaderValueOption::OVERWRITE_IF_EXISTS_OR_ADD, true}; } ``` ([src](https://github.com/envoyproxy/envoy/pull/42878/changes#diff-c21d3f52d2ab9b9b72049be64abc353ac630adfd5729d376d7f32e6ba5897e76R33-R40)) *Repro steps*: 1. Switch to v1.37 2. In ext_authz server mutate headers but do not set `append` nor `append_action`. Result: Headers are appended anyway. Clients can easily fix this by explicitly setting the `append` or `append_action` fields, but this is breaking behaviour from the previous version and should at least be documented as such if not rectified.
envoyproxy/envoy#43310.title: ext-authz: New `append_action` introduces issue where headers are erroneously appended

To be clear, it's [filters.http.golang.v3alpha.Config](https://www.envoyproxy.io/docs/envoy/latest/api-v3/extensions/filters/http/golang/v3alpha/golang.proto#extensions-filters-http-golang-v3alpha-config)

*Logs*:

When using `envoyproxy/envoy:contrib-distroless-v1.37-latest`, I receive the following error message:
```
[source/extensions/config_subscription/filesystem/filesystem_subscription_impl.cc:103] Filesystem config update failure: in /etc/envoy/lds.yaml, Unable to parse JSON as proto (INVALID_ARGUMENT: could not find @type 'type.googleapis.com/envoy.extensions.filters.http.golang.v3alpha.Config')
```

envoyproxy/envoy#43396.body:
When using DNS filter access logs with grpc access logger and custom tags using substitution %QUERY_NAME% following error is produced:
> delta config for type.googleapis.com/envoy.config.listener.v3.Listener rejected: Error adding/updating listener(s) listener: Not supported field in StreamInfo: QUERY_NAME


In the gRPC access logs, when [custom tags are created](https://github.com/envoyproxy/envoy/blob/main/source/extensions/access_loggers/grpc/grpc_access_log_utils.cc#L40C25-L47) the command parser isnt passed down (ignored [here](https://github.com/envoyproxy/envoy/blob/main/source/extensions/access_loggers/grpc/http_config.cc#L24) when passed, [createCustomTag takes command parsers](https://github.com/envoyproxy/envoy/blob/main/source/common/tracing/custom_tag_impl.cc#L185-L186)). When validation is done to ensure commands in custom tags exist, it is effectively only looking at globally registered built in command parsers. This results in DNS filter (or any non tcp proxy and http conn man filter) commands not being recognized leading to validation failures as DNS filter access log commands arent globally registered.
envoyproxy/envoy#43396.title: access_logs: DNS filter access logs using custom tags in gRPC access logs fails validation

envoyproxy/envoy#43398.body:
*Title*: *Add option to disable appending the original path to the auth service path - http ext_authz*

---

*Description*:

I came across the following while trying to use envoy-gateway.

When using [extAuth](https://gateway.envoyproxy.io/docs/api/extension_types/#extauth) of type [http](https://gateway.envoyproxy.io/docs/api/extension_types/#httpextauthservice), which I suppose it uses envoy-proxy's [http ext_authz](https://www.envoyproxy.io/docs/envoy/latest/api-v3/extensions/filters/http/ext_authz/v3/ext_authz.proto#extensions-filters-http-ext-authz-v3-httpservice),  

If the original request path is `/hello`, and the auth service path is `/auth`,  
then the path of the authorization request that envoy-proxy will attempt to use/hit, will be: `/auth/hello` instead of just `/auth`

The relevant **scenario(s) or use case(s)**, where adding an option/capability to disable the path appending behavior would be helpful:

1. [Actual Scenario I Faced Recently] The authorization service is internal, it expects a path such as: `/auth`, any appended extras such as: `/auth/hello` causes a 404, the user does have some control over the authorization service, but a workaround/fix in the service, requires code change, which is not always possible/welcome, and/or might require extended time to get the workaround/fix added.
2. [Hypothetical Scenario, not verified/checked yet] The authorization service is external, it expects a path such as: `/auth`, any appended extras such as: `/auth/hello` cause a 404, the user does not have any control over the external authorization service code base.

All in all, adding such option/capability to disable the path appending behavior in envoy-proxy, would allow envoy-gateway to expose the same options to end users, therefore, **unblocking** the use and adoption of envoy-gateway.

---

Would this be too complicated to add/do?  
Or would you suggest/recommend a different way to achieve this outcome?

---

*Relevant Links*:
1. First opened the issue in envoy-gateway: https://github.com/envoyproxy/gateway/issues/7992#issuecomment-3786433170
2. Relevant resource: https://www.envoyproxy.io/docs/envoy/latest/api-v3/extensions/filters/http/ext_authz/v3/ext_authz.proto#extensions-filters-http-ext-authz-v3-httpservice
3. Relevant resource: https://github.com/envoyproxy/envoy/tree/main/source/extensions/filters/http/ext_authz
4. Perhaps a relevant issue & a hack: https://github.com/envoyproxy/envoy/issues/23360

Thanks.
envoyproxy/envoy#43398.title: Add option to disable appending the original path to the auth service path - http ext_authz

envoyproxy/envoy#43436.body:
*Title*: Allow reading from listener_filter_buffer_ in network filter outside of on_read

*Description*:

Currently, network filters can only read data in on_read (onData)

https://github.com/envoyproxy/envoy/blob/main/source/extensions/dynamic_modules/sdk/rust/src/lib.rs#L4187

However, when returning `StopIteration`, a listener buffer is created to store bytes until the filter is ready to continue

https://github.com/envoyproxy/envoy/blob/273f231b7db55df33cab2721cd16a515741680ce/source/common/listener_manager/active_tcp_socket.cc#L85

It seems it would be possible to allow reading from this outside of on_read, notably on_scheduled. Would it make sense to add this ABI, similar to how HTTP filters can read from both received and buffered bytes?

Note, the main motivation for this is to have some backpressure mechanism for network filters - it may not work as well as HTTP/2 flow control, but if bytes can be left in the socket buffer and read from on_scheduled when needed, I think it would provide some sort of backpressure. I notice the word peek here which makes it seem that the bytes do stay in the socket buffer.

https://github.com/envoyproxy/envoy/blob/273f231b7db55df33cab2721cd16a515741680ce/source/common/listener_manager/active_tcp_socket.cc#L158

If they aren't and it's just an internal buffer, then it would essentially have the same effect to buffer within the filter instead.

/cc @agrawroh @wbpcode @mathetake 

[optional *Relevant Links*:]
>Any extra documentation required to understand the issue.
envoyproxy/envoy#43436.title: Allow reading from listener_filter_buffer_ in network filter outside of on_read

envoyproxy/envoy#43484.body:
*Title*: *Add config to the override_host lb policy to allow adding metadata label with the ip:port of the selected host.*

*Description*:

The [Override Host Load Balancing policy](https://www.envoyproxy.io/docs/envoy/latest/intro/arch_overview/upstream/load_balancing/override_host) allows specifying upstream host(s) via a metadata key or header which will override the results of traditional load blancing for host selection. 

This was added in the context of the [Gateway API Inference Extension](https://gateway-api-inference-extension.sigs.k8s.io/#key-features), to enable the Endpoint Picker (EPP) external processing server to add a metadata key to a request, containing upstream hosts for endpoint selection. The policy is configured to read a set of destination endpoint ip:port pairs from the `x-gateway-destination-endpoint`, which it then uses for host selection, as well as a fallback endpoint picker, in the case that none of the endpoints provided via the metadata key can be selected, or the metadata key is missing.

As part of the [Endpoint Picker Protocol](https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/gateway-api-inference-extension/tree/main/docs/proposals/004-endpoint-picker-protocol#destination-endpoint-served), the Envoy proxy must also communicate back to the EPP which provided endpoint was ultimately used to serve the request, via the `x-gateway-destination-endpoint-served` metadata key in the ext_proc [ProcessingRequest.metadata_context](https://github.com/envoyproxy/envoy/blob/v1.35.0/api/envoy/service/ext_proc/v3/external_processor.proto#L130) field.

We're proposing adding the ability to configure a metadata key for the selected host, such that the load balancer policy will have a simple mechanism to add `x-gateway-destination-endpoint-served` to the `dynamic_metadata` of the request, following host selection.

This EPP protocol detail has not been widely adopted yet amongst Gateway API Inference Extension implementations. Updating the `override_host` LB policy will allow easier adoption without the need to create custom filters in order to populate `dynamic_metadata` to conform with the API specification.

Assigning this to myself.

cc @paul-r-gall 

*Relevant Links*:

[Gateway API Inference Extension](https://gateway-api-inference-extension.sigs.k8s.io/#key-features)
[Endpoint Picker Protocol Proposal](https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/gateway-api-inference-extension/tree/main/docs/proposals/004-endpoint-picker-protocol#destination-endpoint-served)
[Override Host Load Balancing Policy](https://github.com/envoyproxy/go-control-plane/blob/envoy/v1.36.0/envoy/extensions/load_balancing_policies/override_host/v3/override_host.pb.go)
[Related issue in Istio](https://github.com/istio/istio/issues/59006)
envoyproxy/envoy#43484.title: override_host: support config to populate dynamic_metadata with selected host

envoyproxy/envoy#43492.body:
*Title*: *New HTTP filter for extracting metadata from AWS eventstream into dynamic metadata*

*Description*:
In prior PRs, we've added support for parsing SSE, extract contents into dynamic metadata (useful for LLM token usage extraction for ratelimit & observability) and a generic json content parser.
- https://github.com/envoyproxy/envoy/pull/42762
- https://github.com/envoyproxy/envoy/pull/43207
- https://github.com/envoyproxy/envoy/pull/43081

Having ability to extract from SSE, envoy can extract token usage from most LLM providers/inference engines (OpenAI API, Anthropic API, vLLM, TGI, Ollama). 

However, one exception is models served behind AWS Bedrock, the streamed responses are not in SSE format but in AWS eventstream (`application/vnd.amazon.eventstream`). This appears to be a encoding format used by many AWS services and details on the wire format is [here](https://smithy.io/2.0/aws/amazon-eventstream.html). 

I wonder if it makes sense to add decoding/parsing logic for AWS eventstream in `source/extensions/common/aws/eventstream/` perhaps similar to what I added for SSE. And write a new filter `aws_eventstream_to_metadata` HTTP filter for extraction. We may be able to reuse the json content parser for extracting from the json after decoding the eventstream binary. 

Example body after decoding 
```
{
  "completion": "The capital of France is Paris.",
  "stop_reason": "stop_sequence",
  "model": "anthropic.claude-instant-v1",
  "usage": {
    "prompt_tokens": 10,
    "completion_tokens": 7,
    "total_tokens": 17
  }
}
```

[optional *Relevant Links*:]
https://docs.aws.amazon.com/bedrock/latest/APIReference/API_runtime_InvokeModelWithResponseStream.html
envoyproxy/envoy#43492.title: HTTP filter for extracting LLM token usage info from AWS eventstream

envoyproxy/envoy#43507.body:
*Title*: Local Close Reason was not set! error if closing in network filter

*Description*:

If a network filter that is behind an http filter closes the connection, Envoy logs

```
[2026-02-16 14:39:44.004][61155787][error][envoy_bug] [source/common/http/conn_manager_impl.cc:615] envoy bug failure: !local_close_reason.empty(). Details: Local Close Reason was not set!
```

The direct cause of this is not passing details to `connection().close()`

https://github.com/envoyproxy/envoy/blob/main/source/extensions/filters/network/dynamic_modules/abi_impl.cc#L319
https://github.com/envoyproxy/envoy/blob/main/source/common/network/connection_impl.h#L75

There is a long comment about the trickiness of possible double-close on HCM

https://github.com/envoyproxy/envoy/blob/main/source/common/http/conn_manager_impl.cc#L615

So I'm not sure which is better of

- Update dynamic modules to always close with details in case there is an HCM present. If the user didn't provide any, it would need to be empty string or such
- Try to resolve the root issue the comment points out as it indeed is surprising to get a log just because details wasn't passed in a network filter (dynamic modules or not)

I'm guessing we'd go with the former though

/cc @agrawroh @wbpcode

`envoy_filter.close` in a network filter that has an http connection manager after it

*Title*: Peak EWMA load balancer (contrib) randomly segfaults -- (Envoy via Istio-Proxy 1.29.0)

*Description*:

**I certainly understand that the Istio-Proxy is a custom Envoy build and that [Peak EWMA is a contrib module](https://github.com/envoyproxy/envoy/pull/40653) on top of that. I clarified with the envoy-security contact (@ggreenway), that this crash issue is OK to report publicly.**



It seems to me that this NOT related to traffic since I can run load of requests across different upstream targets with no issues.
At first glance it seems like random crashes. Maybe (!) they are related to the constant Pod and target churn happening in Kubernetes cluster. It's just a sandbox cluster, so no production traffic affected. 

But that also means I gladly run more test if they help (you).



*Repro steps*:

1) I wanted to try the contrib loadbalancer module for Peak EWMA, added via PR https://github.com/envoyproxy/envoy/pull/40653 by @rroblak 

2) Since we are running Istio, I proposed to add the PeakEWMA load balancer via https://github.com/istio/proxy/pull/6690, which was merged and is now part of Istio-Proxy >=1.29.0.

3) I upgraded our Istio sandbox to 1.29.0 (which means it runs https://github.com/istio/proxy/releases/tag/1.29.0).

4) Since Peak EWMA is not yet directly configurable via Istio configuration I added an EnvoyFilter which I attached as YAML.

5) After a while I noticed some container restarts and with Envoy crashing and throwing strack traces.


*Config*:


EnvoyFilter 

envoyproxy/envoy#43640.body:
*Title*:  `set_filter_state` can not pull dynamic metadata from `network.ext_authz`

*Description*:

Since `ext_authz` performs the check in the `onData` handle it does not allow interactions with other filters. Currently `set_filter_state` sets filter state in the `onNewConnection` handle, as a consequence it can not interact with the dynamic metadata set by `ext_auth` filter.

An option would be to alter the `set_filter_state` to also perform an action in the `onData` handle. Before https://github.com/envoyproxy/envoy/pull/41696 there were similar problems with the interaction between `ext_authz` with `tcp_proxy`.

Another option would be to add an option to perform the check in the `onNewConnection` handle (aka revive https://github.com/envoyproxy/envoy/pull/40827).

Happy to contribute this!
envoyproxy/envoy#43640.title: `set_filter_state` can not pull dynamic metadata from `network.ext_authz`

envoyproxy/envoy#43642.body:
*Title*: *TLS: add CRL-related information to TRANSPORT_FAILURE_REASON*

*Description*:
Currently, operators are responsible for providing and maintaining CRLs used by Envoy proxy. CRL verification checks may fail due to not-yet-ready/expired CRLs or missing CRLs (e.g. for a new CRLDP not previously known to operators). 

Similar to previous enhancements that included certificate SANs when SAN validation fails, adding certificate CRLDP and CRL-related error information to the `TRANSPORT_FAILURE_REASON` can help operators quickly troubleshoot issues. 

[optional *Relevant Links*:]

- https://github.com/envoyproxy/envoy/issues/31320 
- https://github.com/envoyproxy/envoy/pull/43069
envoyproxy/envoy#43642.title: TLS: add CRL-related information to `TRANSPORT_FAILURE_REASON`

envoyproxy/envoy#43665.body:
### *Description*:
Most of my orgs infra resides in on-premise datacenters with many racks, each of which is treated as an individual locality zone in the context of Envoy. For our large services we use least-request + zone-aware routing to balance traffic while preferring the local rack. This works well when incoming load is roughly uniform between racks/zones, but when it isn't the upstream cluster develops hotspots.

The cause here is that zone-aware routing decides what percentage of traffic to keep local vs remote based on healthy host counts (default) or host weights. It has no visibility into actual backend load. Least-request helps within a zone but can't redistribute across zones.

### *Proposed solution*: load-aware locality_basis mode for zone-aware routing
(Note: this is not the approach I ended up taking, read the later comments.)

Add an `ORCA_UTILIZATION` (open to better names) locality_basis mode to `ZoneAwareLbConfig` that uses ORCA load reports to drive the local-vs-remote split:

1. Aggregate per-zone average utilization from ORCA reports on each host.
2. Compare local zone utilization to the global average.
3. If the local zone is overloaded, spill excess traffic to underloaded remote zones proportional to their available headroom (1 - utilization).
4. (optional) Apply EMA dampening to smooth out ORCA data and prevent oscillation.
5. Support a utilization variance threshold knob — when all zones are within threshold of the global average, snap to local-only routing to avoid unnecessary cross-zone traffic.

This approach converges toward utilization equilibrium across zones while also preserving local preference as much as possible.

### *Prerequisites / related work:*

This mode requires an LB policy that populates ORCA data and accepts localityLbConfig or zoneAwareLbConfig directly. I've started on this in #43623 by:

- Extracting code from client-side weighted round robin into an OrcaWeightManager class that handles ORCA report processing, per-host weight calculation (`weight = qps / utilization`), periodic updates, and median-weight fallback for hosts without ORCA data. This is nearly identical to the existing code and is intended to be reusable across ORCA-aware LB policies.
- Rewriting `wrr_locality` as a standalone ThreadAwareLoadBalancer that accepts full localityLbConfig and creates an OrcaWeightManager, rather than wrapping client-side weighted round robin.

Once the above is added I have a prototype ready for the locality_basis mode and will open a follow-up PR.

#### Why a new locality basis mode vs `HEALTHY_HOSTS_WEIGHT`?

The `OrcaWeightManager` extraction + l`ocalityLbConfig` integration means that `HEALTHY_HOSTS_WEIGHT` locality basis would use ORCA-derived host weights instead for the zone split but it solves a different problem. The ORCA weight formula is `weight = qps / utilization`, which measures host capacity — how much throughput a host can deliver per unit of utilization. For well-behaved backends where utilization scales linearly with QPS, this simplifies to a constant regardless of current load. A host at 80% utilization and a host at 30% utilization with the same hardware get the same weight.

This means `HEALTHY_HOSTS_WEIGHT` is useful for capacity asymmetry (heterogeneous hardware, partially degraded hosts) but is blind to load asymmetry (uneven incoming traffic causing hotspots), which is the primary problem motivating this proposal. `ORCA_UTILIZATION` addresses this by routing based on available headroom (1 - utilization) directly rather than capacity-derived weights.
 
### *Alternative considered*: new ORCA-based LB policy with locality selection

Rather than extending zone-aware routing, a dedicated ORCA-based policy could handle locality selection internally — potentially via a new `locality_picking_policy` alongside `endpoint_picking_policy`. This would give full control over the locality selection algorithm but wouldn't benefit from existing zone-aware routing infrastructure (residual routing, panic thresholds, etc.) and would be a larger design surface.

I believe extending zone-aware routing is the better path since it builds on proven mechanics and may be easier for users to reason about, but I'd like additional input on whether a separate locality selection abstraction has broader value.

Relevant Links:
- https://github.com/envoyproxy/envoy/issues/6614
- https://github.com/envoyproxy/envoy/pull/43623
envoyproxy/envoy#43665.title: Proposal: ORCA-driven locality routing for zone-aware load balancing

envoyproxy/envoy#43674.body:
*Description*:
Coming from [this discussion](https://github.com/envoyproxy/gateway/discussions/8172) it would be nice to be able to add the span id into the access logs.

`%TRACE_ID%` is already available. `%SPAN_ID%` is not available.

As pointed out in the discussion there are two spans so the best way might be to just make both available `%CLIENT_SPAN_ID%` and `%SERVER_SPAN_ID%` so that the user can choose which one to take instead of having to choose in the implementation.
envoyproxy/envoy#43674.title: access_log: add span id

envoyproxy/envoy#43680.body: *Title*: When using a `direct_response` route action with `body_format` (introduced in #41821), the response `Content-Type` header is always `text/plain` regardless of the format configuration *Description*: The `SubstitutionFormatString` API allows setting a `content_type`, defaulting to `text/plain` for a text format and `applicaton/json` for a JSON format. Using this config option in a `direct_response` route action's `body_format` will not result in the route's response having the configured `Content-Type` header. Instead, `text/plain` is always returned. *Repro steps*: 1. Configure a route with `direct_response` action and `body_format` with `content_type: "application/json"` configured 2. Make a GET request to that route 3. Observe that `Content-Type` in the response is `text/plain` *Config*: ```yaml static_resources: listeners: - name: listener_0 address: socket_address: { address: 0.0.0.0, port_value: 10000 } filter_chains: - filters: - name: envoy.filters.network.http_connection_manager typed_config: "@type": type.googleapis.com/envoy.extensions.filters.network.http_connection_manager.v3.HttpConnectionManager stat_prefix: ingress_http codec_type: AUTO route_config: name: local_route virtual_hosts: - name: direct_response domains: ["*"] routes: - match: { prefix: "/" } direct_response: status: 200 body_format: content_type: "text/html" text_format_source: inline_string: "Request Path: %REQ(:path)%" http_filters: - name: envoy.filters.http.router typed_config: "@type": type.googleapis.com/envoy.extensions.filters.http.router.v3.Router ``` I was not sure whether to file this as a bug or a feature request, since the PR implementing this feature (#41821) did not even intend to address this directly. I decided for bug considering that as a user, I generally only see the API surface which does present me with the `content_type` option.
envoyproxy/envoy#43680.title: direct_response: `Content-Type` is always `text/plain` and ignores `body_format` config

*Set filter state in Lua*

*Description*:
The filter state object API supports `log()` and `get()` only. Can it support a `set()` as well? WASM seems to have support for it.

*Relevant Links*:
WASM support: https://github.com/envoyproxy/envoy/issues/28673
Lua docs: https://www.envoyproxy.io/docs/envoy/latest/configuration/http/http_filters/lua_filter#filter-state-object-api

envoyproxy/envoy#43811.body:
*Title*: *Add a local-reply matcher input for custom_response policies*

*Description*:

Today `envoy.filters.http.custom_response` policies are selected via response matchers, but there is no first-class matcher input to reliably distinguish Envoy local replies from upstream responses.

Request: add a dedicated HTTP matcher input to identify local replies(boolean/presence-style), so `LocalResponsePolicy` can be applied only to local replies when desired.

This would let control planes (for example Envoy Gateway route-targeted policies) customize local error responses per route without unintentionally overriding normal upstream responses.

[optional *Relevant Links*:]
>Any extra documentation required to understand the issue.
envoyproxy/envoy#43811.title: Add a local-reply matcher input for custom_response policies

envoyproxy/envoy#43847.body:
Envoy has a powerful stats system that allows users to collect and analyze various metrics about proxy performance and behavior. However, collecting stats introduces overhead, especially when a large number of metrics are involved. To address this, Envoy provides a stats matcher mechanism to selectively enable or disable stats collection.

### Limitations of the Current Approach

The current stats matcher is a **bootstrap-level configuration** that can only be set at Envoy startup and **cannot leverage the xDS API for dynamic updates**. Changing the stats matcher requires restarting Envoy, which causes downtime and disrupts service.

This becomes particularly problematic at scale. For example, if a user has 1,000 clusters but only wants to enable stats for 10 of them, they must enumerate all desired stat patterns in the global matcher — a process that is both tedious and error-prone. Worse, applying any change still requires an Envoy restart.

Here is an example of the current stats matcher configuration in the bootstrap file:

```yaml
stats_config:
  stats_matcher:
    inclusion_list:
      patterns:
        - prefix: "envoy.cluster."
```

## Goals

- Support fine-grained, per-cluster and per-listener stats matcher configuration.
- Enable dynamic updates to stats matcher configuration without restarting Envoy.

## Design: Minimal Changes to the Existing Stats System

The current bootstrap-based stats matcher is evaluated when Envoy creates a new stat. It checks whether the stat name matches the configured patterns and rejects unmatched stats. Here is a simplified code snippet:

```cpp
Counter& ThreadLocalStoreImpl::ScopeImpl::counterFromStatNameWithTags(
    const StatName& name, StatNameTagVectorOptConstRef stat_name_tags) {
  if (parent_.rejectsAll()) {
    return parent_.null_counter_;
  }

  // Determine the final name based on the prefix and the passed name.
  TagUtility::TagStatNameJoiner joiner(prefix_.statName(), name, stat_name_tags, symbolTable());
  Stats::StatName final_stat_name = joiner.nameWithTags();

  StatsMatcher::FastResult fast_reject_result = parent_.fastRejects(final_stat_name);
  if (fast_reject_result == StatsMatcher::FastResult::Rejects) {
    return parent_.null_counter_;
  }

  // ......
}
```

When Envoy loads a new cluster or listener, it calls methods like the one above to create stats. The stats matcher checks whether each stat name matches the configured patterns — matched stats are created; unmatched stats are rejected.

### Proposed Change

To support fine-grained stats matchers, we introduce an **optional, per-resource stats matcher** for clusters and listeners. When Envoy loads a new cluster or listener and creates its stats, it follows this precedence:

1. If a **resource-specific stats matcher** is configured, use it.
2. Otherwise, fall back to the **global (bootstrap) stats matcher**.

This approach requires minimal changes to the existing stats system: we only need to extend the `create` methods of the stats scope to accept an optional stats matcher parameter. The existing global stats matcher continues to work as before, and the new per-resource matchers layer on top of it.

## No API Changes Required

The per-resource stats matcher configuration is carried via **cluster/listener metadata** using the existing `typed_filter_metadata` field. This means users can configure fine-grained stats matchers through the existing xDS API without any proto changes.

Example — enabling stats for a specific cluster:

```yaml
clusters:
  - name: cluster_1
    metadata:
      typed_filter_metadata:
        envoy.stats_matcher:
          "@type": type.googleapis.com/envoy.config.metrics.v3.StatsMatcher
          inclusion_list:
            patterns:
              - prefix: "envoy.cluster.cluster_1."
    connect_timeout: 0.25s
    type: strict_dns
    lb_policy: round_robin
    load_assignment:
      cluster_name: cluster_0
      endpoints:
      - lb_endpoints:
        - endpoint:
            address:
              socket_address:
                address: 127.0.0.1
                port_value: 10001
```

## Why Metadata Instead of a New API Field?

The stats matcher is an Envoy-specific concern. Non-Envoy xDS implementations do not need to be aware of it. Using metadata keeps this configuration out of the core `Cluster` and `Listener` proto definitions, avoiding unnecessary coupling and keeping the xDS API clean for all implementations.
envoyproxy/envoy#43847.title: fine-grained/dynamic stats matcher support

envoyproxy/envoy#43929.body:
*Title*: Given the change to the FIPS bazel pipeline, how might one trigger this flag in a remote repository of Envoy?

*Description*:
Given the following workspace:

```
workspace(name = "custom-proxy")

# See:
# https://github.com/envoyproxy/envoy/blob/main/bazel/README.md#customize-extension-build-config
local_repository(
    name = "envoy_build_config",
    path = "envoy_build_config",
)

local_repository(
    name = "envoy",
    path = "envoy",
)

... load all the envoy deps and bazel mappings....
```

And the following bazelrc:

```
import %workspace%/envoy/.bazelrc

# overrides
build --workspace_status_command="bash envoy/bazel/get_workspace_status"

# Enable FIPS by default for builds

"Can't use the --define flag anymore, but can't set --config?"


build:dev-container --remote_cache=grpc://host.docker.internal:9092
```

How would a user go about triggering the boringssl FIPS pipeline given a submoduled installation of Envoy? The project layout would be as follows:

```
custom-proxy
  BUILD
  WORKSPACE
  .bazelrc
  envoy/<envoys_source_submodule>
  envoy_build_config/
  other_custom_code/
```

Using the `--config=boringssl-fips` flag errors due to the bazel mappings not existing within the custom-proxy build (since they are technically within the @envoy local repository)?

@phlax I know you mentioned to me that 1.38 was bringing quite a few changes towards the transition to bzlmod, any pointers here?
envoyproxy/envoy#43929.title: Building FIPS enabled Envoys using a local bazel repository?

*Title*: Unable to compile Envoy 1.37.1 with FIPS using envoy-build image

*Description*:
We're trying to build Envoy 1.37.1 with the FIPS flag enabled `--define boringssl=fips` but it's failing with the following error while executing the target `@@boringssl_fips//:ninja_bin`

```
Build failed:
  ./ninja: error while loading shared libraries: libc++.so.1: cannot open shared object file: No such file or directory
```

We are using the image `envoyproxy/envoy-build:86873047235e9b8232df989a5999b9bebf9db69c@sha256:20656853fae51927cda557e7af80ccff175f5de6f84bd0f092cd8672b2a6e0fe` to execute the build, this image does not contain `libc++.so.1`.

Are we missing something or that dep is missing from the envoy-build image? This only happens with the FIPS flag, a no-FIPS build runs ok.

envoyproxy/envoy#44035.body:
Currently, you need a workaround like following, if you want to do routing base on the filter state.
```yaml
       - name: set downstream protocol
          typedConfig:
            '@type': type.googleapis.com/envoy.extensions.filters.http.set_filter_state.v3.Config
            onRequestHeaders:
            - factoryKey: envoy.string
              formatString:
                textFormatSource:
                  inlineString: '%PROTOCOL%'
              objectKey: eg.downstream_protocol
              sharedWithUpstream: ONCE
              skipIfEmpty: true
        - name: clear route cache
          typedConfig:
            '@type': type.googleapis.com/envoy.extensions.filters.http.lua.v3.Lua
            defaultSourceCode:
              inlineString: |
                function envoy_on_request(handle)
                  handle:clearRouteCache()
                end
```

cc @wbpcode

envoyproxy/envoy#44114.body:
*Description*:
After 1.36 release. Most likely caused by 369ace259c.
HCM with internal_redirect_policy cannot process requests larger than request_body_buffer_limit, while previous versions were able to stream such requests upstream.
*Repro steps*:
in our case HTTP/1.1 -> EnvoyA(1.36) ->HTTP2-> EnvoyB(1.35) ->HTTP/1.1-> target
EnvoyA is default passthrough HTTP proxy with internal_redirect_policy, with default configuration buffer limit is 1MB.
POST HTTP requests larger than 1MB stuck, incompletely delivered and timeout through upstream.

*Config*:
```
                      routes:
                        - match:
                            prefix: "/"
                          route:
                            cluster: upstream
                            retry_policy:
                              retry_on: "connect-failure,refused-stream,reset-before-request"
                              num_retries: 3
                            internal_redirect_policy:
                              max_internal_redirects: 5
                              redirect_response_codes: [307]
```
envoyproxy/envoy#44114.title: envoy-1.36+: request_body_buffer_limit with internal_redirect_policy regression

openzipkin/zipkin#1754.body:
Similar to how last year, we had users requesting for [Google Stackdriver compatibility](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/stackdriver-zipkin), there are users [explicitly requesting Amazon X-Ray](https://github.com/spring-cloud/spring-cloud-sleuth/issues/459), as well general support and interest from @abhiksingh (X-Ray product lead), and indirect comments which I can't currently find about tension between lambda and zipkin architecture*.

There's no doubt that Zipkin and Amazon interop have been important in the past. Many of our core team rely on AWS and/or make custom components for AWS. This issue will explore how we could fit-in, and how we could allow 3rd party tracers designed for B3 to have the smallest impact to support X-Ray.

Similar to StackDriver, there's two major concerns: propagation and out-of-band data.

Unlike StackDriver, propagation is wider than the X-Ray service. For example, in AWS the same propagation format is used even when X-Ray isn't: ELB uses the same format eventhough ELB doesn't write to X-Ray. There are also interesting concerns such as that Api Gateway restarts traces at its edge, also in X-Ray format. These types of concerns weren't present when we integrated with StackDriver, although they are building in the new [trace-context](https://github.com/TraceContext/tracecontext-spec) format, targeted initially inside Google to StackDriver and gRPC services.

Also unlike StackDriver, the trace ID requires a 32-bit timestamp. This has some impact, as if there's an invalid timestamp, the service will drop any related data. For this reason, pragmatic ID generation strategy is something we must consider. For example, creating "interop" IDs by default where the first 32-bits are a timestamp and the following are 96-bits of random. This discussion occurred at the end of #1262.

Reporting is very much like what we did in StackDriver. The X-Ray format is span structured, with an api to post data to. While it has more structure than Zipkin data, mapping rules like exist in StackDriver are more than possible. Zipkin-compatible (or otherwise) tracers could send data to X-Ray's daemon (automatically present in lambda), the X-Ray POST api, or to a zipkin destination that does one of the two.

The above is fairly reliable from initial explorations, but could change with experience. This issue will track the exploration and any related issues on Zipkin's side.

* If you are running a lambda (serverless) architecture, running a zipkin server, even a stupid proxy to X-Ray one, could feel heavyweight. In this case, writing directly to X-Ray, or translating zipkin to AWS via a kinesis lambda could make sense.
openzipkin/zipkin#1754.title: Amazon X-Ray interop
